Party_Details.Name: Lim Suat Hua

Facts.Text: With effect from 1 September 2007, Lim Suat Hua began employment with Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
On 1 September 2007, a service agreement titled "Lim's Service Agreement" was entered into between Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd and Lim Suat Hua.
Under Lim's Service Agreement dated 1 September 2007, Lim Suat Hua was appointed as Executive Director of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
Clause 4.1 of Lim's Service Agreement dated 1 September 2007 provided for a monthly salary of S$60,000 to be paid to Lim Suat Hua.

Clause 5.1 of Lim's Service Agreement dated 1 September 2007 provided that Lim Suat Hua was entitled to 30 business days of leave per year, not carry-forwardable, to be taken at times approved by the board.
Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd was incorporated on 9 March 2006 to develop a medical complex at No 1 Farrer Park Station Road known as the Connexion project.
At incorporation, Lim Suat Hua was one of the initial shareholders and directors of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
On 1 September 2007, Djeng Shih Kien was appointed Executive Chairman of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd under a service agreement similar in structure to Lim's Service Agreement.
Clause 4.1 of Djeng Shih Kien's service agreement dated 1 September 2007 provided for a monthly salary of S$70,000.

On 29 June 2007, an Investment Agreement was executed providing for investment into Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd by a group of medical doctors or their corporate vehicles referred to as the Investors Group, Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd, and Wharton Scott Pte Ltd.

On 26 September 2007, pursuant to the Investment Agreement, the Investors Group, Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd and Wharton Scott Pte Ltd were registered as new shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.

On 26 September 2007, directors nominated by the new shareholders were appointed to the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd: Maurice Choo, Richard Chew and Whang Hwee Yong for the Investors Group; Dato' Lee Kok Chuan and Dato' Tan Yeong Ching with Ng Chee Yau as alternate director for Berjaya; and Tan Soon Hoe and Richard Charles Helfer for Wharton Scott.

At the conclusion of a board of directors meeting on 21 March 2009, Djeng Shih Kien agreed to step down from his position as Executive Chairman of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd on the basis that Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd would not pursue claims against him.

On 21 March 2009, the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd resolved to set up a Remuneration Committee to review directors' fees and directors' remuneration, including retrospective review of past directors' remuneration.

On 30 March 2009, the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd resolved that Lim Suat Hua be offered a New Service Agreement for the period from 1 April 2009 to the Temporary Occupation Permit of the project at a monthly basic salary of S$50,000, with all other terms of Lim's Service Agreement remaining the same.
On or about 26 March 2009, Maurice Choo verbally informed Lim Suat Hua that her salary of S$60,000 was considered excessive and proposed that her monthly salary be reduced to S$50,000.

On or about 27 March 2009, Lim Suat Hua met Maurice Choo at his clinic, and an electronic diary entry created by him for that date recorded that she accepted an arrangement discussed the previous day, would submit her resignation, and would accept a salary of S$50,000 per month.
At the Annual General Meeting of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd on 2 May 2009, Maurice Choo informed shareholders that Lim Suat Hua had tendered her resignation on 21 March 2009.
On 2 May 2009, draft minutes of the 30 March 2009 board meeting, including a resolution offering Lim Suat Hua a New Service Agreement at S$50,000 per month, were sent to Lim.

On or about 13 April 2009, Lim Suat Hua co-signed an authorisation letter to DBS Bank mandating payment to herself of S$59,662 for April 2009, representing a S$60,000 salary less S$338 in statutory contributions.
From May 2009 to June 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd paid Lim Suat Hua a monthly salary of S$50,000 instead of S$60,000.

Between May 2009 and June 2010, Lim Suat Hua co-signed monthly authorisation letters on behalf of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd to instruct DBS Bank to pay her a net monthly salary of S$49,662, being S$50,000 less statutory deductions.
Between May 2009 and June 2010, Lim Suat Hua prepared a revised budget for Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd in which her monthly salary was recorded as S$50,000.
Between 1 January 2010 and 25 July 2010, Lim Suat Hua took 27 days of leave from her employment with Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.

On 24 November 2009, an email was sent on behalf of Lim Suat Hua to the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd requesting approval to carry forward her 2009 leave, and no director gave consent in response.
From 1 to 3 October 2008, Lim Suat Hua travelled to Thailand on a trip described as the Thailand trip, with expenses borne by Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
On 11 March 2009, a report on hospital software referred to as the Amalga Report, prepared by Lim Kah Chuan, was emailed to the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.

From 20 to 24 October 2008, Lim Suat Hua travelled to New Zealand on a trip described as the New Zealand trip, organised by Beca Carter Holling & Ferner Ltd, together with Djeng Shih Kien, representatives from DP Architects and a representative from Beca Carter.
On 6 November 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd signed a contract with Microsoft Amalga to obtain the Amalga software for the Connexion project.
On 23 March 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd signed a contract with Beca Carter Holling & Ferner Ltd appointing that firm as its mechanical and electrical engineers.
For the financial period from 1 July 2007 to 31 December 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd's audited accounts included an item for travelling and entertainment expenses.

On 18 December 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Facility Agreement, a Profit Sharing Agreement, a Security Trust Agreement and a Deed of Subordination with United Overseas Bank Limited.
On 27 December 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Building Agreement with the Urban Redevelopment Authority of Singapore.
On 24 March 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Hotel Technical Services Agreement with Rendezvous Hotels International Pte Ltd.
In or around 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a tenancy in which Fidelio Realty Pte Ltd acted as a co-broker and received a fee of S$4,935.
Since 7 April 2000, Lim Suat Hua has been a director of Wizvision Pte Ltd, a company providing information technology services.
From 7 December 2007 to 2 August 2009, Lim Kong Wee, son of Lim Suat Hua, was an alternate director of Wizvision Pte Ltd.
With effect from 3 August 2009, Lim Kong Wee became a director of Wizvision Pte Ltd.
In November 2007, Wizvision Pte Ltd entered into an Office/Server Desktop Solution contract with Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
In January 2008, Wizvision Pte Ltd entered into an IT System Maintenance Support contract with Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd for the period 1 April 2008 to 31 March 2009.
On 1 April 2009, Wizvision Pte Ltd entered into an IT System Maintenance Support contract with Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd for the period 1 April 2009 to 31 March 2010.
As at the material times, Lim Suat Hua held a 17% direct shareholding in Wizvision Pte Ltd and a 15.59% indirect shareholding in Wizvision Pte Ltd through Hua Jie Pte Ltd.
At the material times, Lim Wee Kiat, a son of Lim Suat Hua, held an indirect 0.12% stake in Wizvision Pte Ltd through Hua Jie Pte Ltd.
At the material times, Lim Suat Hua and her son Lim Kong Wee were shareholders of Fidelio Realty Pte Ltd.
On 12 July 2010, a Settlement and Share Purchase Agreement referred to as the Settlement Agreement was executed between the two factions of shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
Under the Settlement Agreement dated 12 July 2010, Lim Suat Hua, Djeng Shih Kien and Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd agreed to exit as shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
Under the Settlement Agreement dated 12 July 2010, the last day of employment of Lim Suat Hua as Executive Director of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd was set as 25 July 2010.
Following the termination of her employment on 25 July 2010, a disagreement arose between Lim Suat Hua and Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd over the amount of salary said to be owing to her.
On 22 October 2010, Lim Suat Hua commenced an action against Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd claiming amounts she said were owing under Lim's Service Agreement.

In the course of settlement negotiations in 2010 leading up to the Settlement Agreement, Lim Suat Hua, through her solicitor, sought compensation of S$600,000 representing 12 months of a S$50,000 monthly salary.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, prior to the dispute, the parties' conduct and communications resulted in Lim Suat Hua being paid and treated as entitled to a monthly salary of S$50,000 instead of S$60,000 from April 2009 onwards, and how her salary and leave entitlements up to 25 July 2010 should be quantified.
Party_Details.Rule: In employment and company law, an employee's remuneration and benefits are governed by the express terms of the service contract, any valid variations agreed between the contracting parties, and any applicable implied terms. A contractual variation generally requires agreement between the parties, which may be evidenced by words or conduct. In some circumstances, where parties act over time on a common factual assumption about the terms of their relationship, the law of estoppel by convention may prevent a party from later denying that assumption if it would be unjust to do so. Contractual provisions on annual leave typically limit entitlement to a fixed number of days per year, may prohibit carrying forward unused leave without approval, and may allow employers to adjust salary where leave taken exceeds entitlement.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute documents and conduct Lim's Service Agreement initially set her salary at S$60,000 per month with 30 business days of non-carry-forwardable annual leave. After new investors and directors joined, concerns were raised about the level and approval of senior executives' remuneration, leading to the formation of a Remuneration Committee and a board resolution on 30 March 2009 offering Lim a New Service Agreement at S$50,000 per month from 1 April 2009. Around the same period, Maurice Choo communicated to Lim that her salary was considered excessive and proposed a reduction, and his diary entry records that she would accept S$50,000 per month as part of a broader arrangement. From May 2009 to June 2010, Singapore HealthPartners consistently paid Lim S$50,000 per month, and Lim co-signed bank authorisation letters and prepared a revised budget reflecting a S$50,000 salary without raising objections about a shortfall. During 2010 settlement discussions, Lim's own compensation proposal was framed using a S$50,000 monthly figure. These pre-dispute actions and records , in practice, both sides operated on the basis of a S$50,000 monthly salary from at least May 2009. The leave records Lim took 27 days of leave between 1 January 2010 and 25 July 2010, while her contract provided for 30 days per full year with no automatic carry-forward, and her request to carry forward earlier leave was not approved by the board. These facts frame the later dispute over whether she could claim additional salary based on the original S$60,000 figure and how to account for leave taken in excess of a pro-rated entitlement.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed whether there was a binding variation of Lim's Service Agreement and whether estoppel by convention applied. He found that Lim did not agree on 27 March 2009 to a simple reduction of salary from S$60,000 to S$50,000 as a standalone variation, interpreting Maurice Choo's diary entry as referring to a broader arrangement involving Lim's resignation and a new service agreement. He also considered the 30 March 2009 board minutes and concluded that the resolution only recorded an offer of a New Service Agreement and did not evidence Lim's acceptance. The judge expressed doubts about the completeness and reliability of the audio recordings and transcripts of the 30 March 2009 board meeting and declined to rely heavily on them. Despite finding no express contractual variation, he held that an estoppel by convention arose from May 2009 onwards because both parties acted on a shared assumption that Lim's salary had been reduced to S$50,000. He relied on Lim's failure to object to the reduced payments, her co-signing of bank authorisation letters at the S$50,000 level, her preparation of a revised budget reflecting S$50,000, and her use of S$50,000 as the reference salary in settlement negotiations. Applying the elements of estoppel by convention from Travista Development Pte Ltd v Tan Kim Swee Augustine, he concluded that it would be unjust and unconscionable to allow Lim to revert to the S$60,000 figure for the period May 2009 to June 2010, and therefore dismissed her S$140,000 short-paid salary claim. Because the estoppel only arose from May 2009, he held that Lim did not overpay herself by S$10,000 in April 2009 and rejected the defendant's counterclaim on that point. On leave, he implied a term into the Service Agreement allowing the employer to recover salary corresponding to leave taken in excess of entitlement, reasoning that otherwise an employee could take a full year's leave after only a short period of service and then resign without consequence. He rejected Lim's contention that she could carry forward leave from 2008 and 2009, finding no credible evidence of the alleged Leave Letter and noting that her 24 November 2009 email request to carry forward leave received no board approval. He therefore treated her 2008 and 2009 excess leave as forfeited under clause 5.1 and calculated that for 2010 she was only entitled to 17 pro-rated days of leave up to 25 July 2010, so that ten days were taken in excess. He credited the employer with S$16,438.36 for those ten days and determined that Lim's pro-rated July 2010 salary, based on a S$50,000 monthly rate, was S$23,884.22. On the counterclaims, he interpreted clause 10.7 of the Settlement Agreement as an effective waiver, release and discharge by the company and all shareholders of claims against Lim arising from specified Disclosed Transactions and acts or omissions with respect to those transactions. He held that the Thailand and New Zealand trips, although not themselves specifically referred to in the audited accounts, were acts with respect to Disclosed Transactions, namely the Microsoft Amalga Agreement and the M&E Agreement with Beca Carter, because the trips and the Amalga Report were causally linked to the eventual contracts. Accordingly, he dismissed the counterclaims relating to the alleged unauthorised trips. Regarding Wizvision, he found that Lim had not fully disclosed her indirect 15.59% interest through Hua Jie Pte Ltd but held that clause 10.7 released her from liability in relation to the first two Wizvision contracts, which were referred to in the audited accounts up to 31 December 2008. He concluded that the third Wizvision maintenance contract, effective from 1 April 2009 to 31 March 2010, was outside the scope of clause 10.7 because it was not referred to in the latest audited accounts at the time of the Settlement Agreement. He therefore ordered an account and inquiry before the Registrar into Lim's profits from the third Wizvision contract and payment to Singapore HealthPartners of sums found due, with interest. In summary, the court allowed Lim's claim only to the extent of S$23,884.22 for pro-rated July 2010 salary with interest, dismissed her short-paid salary claim, rejected the employer's counterclaims for the alleged April 2009 overpayment and the Thailand and New Zealand trips, dismissed the counterclaim relating to the first two Wizvision contracts under the release in clause 10.7, and allowed the counterclaim in respect of the third Wizvision contract subject to an account and inquiry.

Party_Details.Name: Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd was incorporated on 9 March 2006 to develop a medical complex comprising a hospital, medical suites and a hotel at No 1 Farrer Park Station Road known as the Connexion project.

On 29 June 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd executed an Investment Agreement with a group of medical doctors or their corporate vehicles referred to as the Investors Group, Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd and Wharton Scott Pte Ltd.

On 26 September 2007, pursuant to the Investment Agreement, the Investors Group, Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd and Wharton Scott Pte Ltd were registered as new shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
On 26 September 2007, directors nominated by the new shareholders were appointed to the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd to represent their respective interests.
On 1 September 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd appointed Lim Suat Hua as Executive Director under a written service agreement.
On 1 September 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd appointed Djeng Shih Kien as Executive Chairman under a written service agreement.
Under Lim's Service Agreement dated 1 September 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd agreed to pay Lim Suat Hua a monthly salary of S$60,000 and to grant 30 business days of leave per year.
Under Djeng's Service Agreement dated 1 September 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd agreed to pay Djeng Shih Kien a monthly salary of S$70,000.

By 21 March 2009, several new directors of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd had expressed dissatisfaction with the timing, approval process and remuneration levels in the service agreements of Lim Suat Hua and Djeng Shih Kien.

On 21 March 2009, the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd resolved to establish a Remuneration Committee to review directors' fees and directors' remuneration, including retrospective review of past directors' remuneration.
At the 21 March 2009 board meeting, Djeng Shih Kien agreed to step down as Executive Chairman of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd on the basis that the company would not pursue claims against him.
On or about 26 March 2009, acting as interim chairman, Maurice Choo informed Lim Suat Hua that her salary of S$60,000 was considered excessive and proposed a reduction to S$50,000 per month.

On or about 27 March 2009, Maurice Choo recorded in his electronic diary that Lim Suat Hua accepted an arrangement discussed the previous day, would submit her resignation, and would accept a salary of S$50,000 per month.

On 30 March 2009, the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd resolved to offer Lim Suat Hua a New Service Agreement from 1 April 2009 to the Temporary Occupation Permit of the project at a monthly basic salary of S$50,000, with all other terms of her existing service agreement unchanged.

On or about 13 April 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd submitted to DBS Bank an authorisation letter co-signed by Lim Suat Hua mandating payment of S$59,662 to her for April 2009, representing a S$60,000 salary less S$338 in statutory contributions.
From May 2009 to June 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd paid Lim Suat Hua a monthly salary of S$50,000.

Between May 2009 and June 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd used monthly authorisation letters co-signed by Lim Suat Hua to instruct DBS Bank to pay her a net salary of S$49,662 per month, being S$50,000 less statutory deductions.
Between May 2009 and June 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd operated on a revised budget prepared by Lim Suat Hua in which her monthly salary was recorded as S$50,000.
At the Annual General Meeting of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd on 2 May 2009, Maurice Choo informed shareholders that Lim Suat Hua had tendered her resignation on 21 March 2009.

From July 2009 onwards, the directors and shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd were divided into two factions, one comprising Djeng Shih Kien, Lim Suat Hua and the Berjaya directors, and the other comprising the Investors Group directors and the Wharton Scott directors.
From mid-2009, disputes between the two factions of shareholders and directors of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd led to several arbitral and court proceedings.

By 12 July 2010, the two factions of shareholders of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd agreed that one side would buy out the other, leading to the execution of the Settlement and Share Purchase Agreement.

On 12 July 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into the Settlement and Share Purchase Agreement under which Lim Suat Hua, Djeng Shih Kien and Berjaya Leisure (Cayman) Ltd exited as shareholders and Lim's last day of employment as Executive Director was set as 25 July 2010.
On 25 July 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd and Lim Suat Hua mutually terminated her employment as Executive Director pursuant to the Settlement Agreement.

After Lim's employment ended on 25 July 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd and Lim disagreed on the amount of salary payable to her, including the basis for calculating her pro-rated July 2010 salary and any adjustments for leave.
On 22 October 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd became the defendant in an action commenced by Lim Suat Hua for unpaid salary under her service agreement.
From 1 to 3 October 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd funded a trip by Lim Suat Hua to Thailand described as the Thailand trip.

From 20 to 24 October 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd funded a trip by Lim Suat Hua to New Zealand described as the New Zealand trip, organised by its mechanical and electrical engineering consultants Beca Carter Holling & Ferner Ltd.
On 11 March 2009, the Amalga Report on hospital software was emailed to the board of Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd.
On 6 November 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd signed a contract with Microsoft Amalga to obtain the Amalga software for the Connexion project.
On 23 March 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd signed a mechanical and electrical engineering contract with Beca Carter Holling & Ferner Ltd.
In its audited financial statements for the period 1 July 2007 to 31 December 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd recorded travelling and entertainment expenses as a category of expenditure.
In November 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into an Office/Server Desktop Solution contract with Wizvision Pte Ltd for information technology services.
In January 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into an IT System Maintenance Support contract with Wizvision Pte Ltd for the period 1 April 2008 to 31 March 2009.
On 1 April 2009, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into an IT System Maintenance Support contract with Wizvision Pte Ltd for the period 1 April 2009 to 31 March 2010.

At the time of the Wizvision contracts, Lim Suat Hua was a director and shareholder of Wizvision Pte Ltd, holding a 17% direct stake and a 15.59% indirect stake through Hua Jie Pte Ltd, and her sons held directorship and a small indirect stake in Wizvision.

In or around 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a tenancy in which Fidelio Realty Pte Ltd acted as co-broker and received a fee of S$4,935, while Lim Suat Hua and her son Lim Kong Wee were shareholders of Fidelio.

On 18 December 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Facility Agreement, Profit Sharing Agreement, Security Trust Agreement and Deed of Subordination with United Overseas Bank Limited.
On 27 December 2007, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Building Agreement with the Urban Redevelopment Authority of Singapore.
On 24 March 2008, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd entered into a Hotel Technical Services Agreement with Rendezvous Hotels International Pte Ltd.

The Settlement Agreement executed on 12 July 2010 contained clause 10.7, under which the company and each shareholder agreed to waive, release and discharge Lim Suat Hua from claims arising from specified Disclosed Transactions and acts or omissions with respect to those transactions.

During settlement negotiations in 2010 leading to the Settlement Agreement, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd and Lim Suat Hua exchanged emails through their solicitors in which Lim's compensation proposal was based on a S$50,000 monthly salary.

By 2010, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd had become aware that Lim Suat Hua had not fully disclosed her indirect 15.59% interest in Wizvision Pte Ltd through Hua Jie Pte Ltd in relation to the Wizvision contracts.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and agreements, Singapore HealthPartners Pte Ltd was obliged to pay Lim Suat Hua salary at S$60,000 or S$50,000 per month from April 2009 to July 2010, how her pro-rated July 2010 salary and leave adjustments should be calculated, and whether Singapore HealthPartners could pursue monetary claims against Lim in respect of overseas trips and Wizvision-related transactions in light of the Settlement Agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: In contract and company law, an employer's payment obligations are determined by the express terms of the service contract as varied by any subsequent agreement or by doctrines such as estoppel by convention, which can prevent a party from denying a shared factual assumption where both parties have acted on it and it would be unjust to resile. Annual leave entitlements are governed by the contract and may be implied terms allowing recovery of salary corresponding to leave taken in excess of entitlement. Directors who have interests in transactions with their company are generally disclose those interests, and companies may seek to recover profits obtained in connection with such positions unless the company and its shareholders validly release or ratify the conduct. A settlement agreement can operate as a contractual waiver and release of existing and potential claims within its defined scope, including claims arising from specified transactions and related acts or omissions.
Party_Details.Application: Singapore HealthPartners initially agreed to pay Lim S$60,000 per month under her 2007 service agreement. After new investors and directors joined, concerns about executive remuneration led to the formation of a Remuneration Committee and a board resolution on 30 March 2009 offering Lim a New Service Agreement at S$50,000 per month from 1 April 2009. Around the same time, the interim chairman communicated to Lim that her salary was considered excessive and proposed a reduction, and his diary entry recorded that she would accept S$50,000 per month as part of a broader arrangement. From May 2009 to June 2010, the company consistently paid Lim S$50,000 per month, used bank authorisation letters reflecting that amount, and operated on a revised budget prepared by Lim that recorded her salary as S$50,000. Lim did not raise objections to the reduced payments during this period and later framed her settlement compensation proposal using the S$50,000 figure. These facts the company and Lim conducted their relationship on the basis of a S$50,000 monthly salary from at least May 2009, which later supported the application of estoppel by convention to limit Lim's claim for additional salary. Regarding leave, the company relied on clause 5.1 of Lim's Service Agreement, which granted 30 business days of leave per year with no carry-forward, and on the absence of board approval for Lim's 2009 carry-forward request, to treat her 2008 and 2009 excess leave as forfeited. The company calculated that Lim took 27 days of leave between 1 January 2010 and 25 July 2010, exceeding a pro-rated entitlement of 17 days, and used the value of ten days' salary as a credit against her July 2010 pay. For its counterclaims, Singapore HealthPartners pointed to the Thailand and New Zealand trips funded by the company and to Lim's roles and shareholdings in Wizvision and Fidelio in relation to contracts those entities had with the company. However, clause 10.7 of the Settlement Agreement, executed by the company and all shareholders, defined Disclosed Transactions and provided a broad waiver and release of claims arising from those transactions and related acts or omissions. The Microsoft Amalga Agreement, the M&E Agreement with Beca Carter, and the first two Wizvision contracts were within the Disclosed Transactions or referred to in the audited accounts up to 31 December 2008, bringing associated acts within the scope of the release. The third Wizvision maintenance contract, effective from 1 April 2009 to 31 March 2010, was not referred to in those audited accounts and therefore fell outside the contractual release, leaving room for the company to seek an account of profits in relation to that contract.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Singapore HealthPartners had not obtained Lim's express agreement on 27 March 2009 to a simple reduction of salary as a standalone variation of her service agreement, but nonetheless held that an estoppel by convention arose from May 2009 because both parties acted on the shared assumption that her salary had been reduced to S$50,000. The judge relied on the company's consistent payment of S$50,000, Lim's co-signing of bank authorisation letters and preparation of a revised budget at that level, and her use of S$50,000 in settlement negotiations, and concluded that it would be unconscionable to allow her to claim S$60,000 for the period May 2009 to June 2010. This finding effectively limited the company's salary obligation to S$50,000 per month from May 2009 onward and defeated Lim's S$140,000 short-paid salary claim. The judge also held that the estoppel did not apply to April 2009, so the company's counterclaim that Lim had overpaid herself S$10,000 for that month failed. On leave, the court implied a term into the service agreement allowing the company to recover salary corresponding to leave taken in excess of entitlement, reasoning that otherwise an employee could exploit the leave provision by taking a full year's leave after a short period of service and then resigning. The judge rejected Lim's assertion of an oral agreement and a Leave Letter permitting carry-forward of 2008 leave, noting the absence of the letter in pleadings, the lack of discovery, and the failure to call Djeng as a witness, as well as the absence of any reference to such a letter in Lim's 24 November 2009 email requesting carry-forward of 2009 leave. He found that no director had consented to that request and treated her 2008 and 2009 excess leave as forfeited under clause 5.1. He accepted the company's calculation that Lim was entitled to only 17 pro-rated days of leave for 2010 up to 25 July 2010 and had taken 27 days, resulting in ten days of excess leave valued at S$16,438.36, which he credited against her July 2010 salary. He then calculated her pro-rated July 2010 salary on a S$50,000 monthly basis and arrived at a net amount of S$23,884.22 payable by the company, with interest. On the counterclaims, the judge interpreted clause 10.7 of the Settlement Agreement as a broad and unconditional release by the company and all shareholders of claims against Lim arising from Disclosed Transactions and related acts or omissions. He rejected the company's argument that the Thailand and New Zealand trips were covered merely as travelling and entertainment expenses in the audited accounts but held that they were nonetheless acts with respect to Disclosed Transactions because the Thailand trip and Amalga Report were causally linked to the Microsoft Amalga Agreement and the New Zealand trip was linked to the M&E Agreement with Beca Carter. As a result, he found that clause 10.7 barred the company's counterclaims relating to the alleged unauthorised trips. Regarding Wizvision, the judge accepted that Lim had not fully disclosed her indirect 15.59% interest through Hua Jie Pte Ltd but held that the first two Wizvision contracts were within the scope of clause 10.7 because they were referred to in the audited accounts up to 31 December 2008. He concluded that the Settlement Agreement, signed by all shareholders, was intended to wipe the slate clean and that the release in clause 10.7 applied even if the shareholders were not fully informed of Lim's partial non-disclosure at the time. He therefore dismissed the counterclaim in respect of the first two Wizvision contracts. However, he found that the third Wizvision maintenance contract, effective from 1 April 2009 to 31 March 2010, was not referred to in the audited accounts available when the Settlement Agreement was signed and thus fell outside the release. For that contract, he ordered an account and inquiry before the Registrar into Lim's profits and payment to Singapore HealthPartners of sums found due, with interest. Overall, the court partially allowed Lim's claim only for S$23,884.22 and largely rejected the company's monetary counterclaims, except for granting relief in principle on the third Wizvision contract subject to an account.
